track_listing.details: White Punks On Dope; LP-Nina Hagen Band

transcript.text: On 275 and 285 and in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Well, hello there. This is Tommy Vance. Welcome to the Friday Rock Show. Tonight, two classic sessions by Roxy Music.
Their first ever broadcast from 1972 and another one from 1973.
We've got classic cuts by Queen and Lynyrd Skynyrd, among others, and new tracks by Motörhead, Patti Smith, Joe Walsh, Vangelis, Elvis Costello, Link Wray and Nina Hagen.
There's the Friday Night Connection, three pieces of music with a theme
that might win you a £10 record token.
But tonight we start with Carlos Santana, and this one is for the wife.
Nine minutes after ten o'clock, that is Motorhead and the track called Overkill.
I beg your pardon, the track called Damage Case.
I think I'm suffering from it, from the album called Overkill.
And before that, Carlos Santana and a track from an album entitled Inner Secrets
that came out some time ago.
The track was Open Invitation.
And it was for Paul and Neil and Samantha and Karen,
and also for Gary, who had a birthday about nine days ago, I think it was, nine or ten days ago,
and for Rona and Barbara and Ronnie Veal, who's better known as God, I'm told.
And also, if I can dedicate that to some friends of mine called Rod and Piers and Tom, or rather Tony and Tim,
and everybody who lives opposite the Scrubs, not in it, and works for Shaw Nichols Associates.
The Motorhead track was for Diane and Helmut and Sandra and Graham, who live in Harlow,
and also for Keith Poulton, who lives in Streakley.
The Patti Smith Group.
One of the most inventive groups in the world currently on tour in Japan.
That was Queen from the 1974 album Sheer Heart Attack and Brighton Rock.
Especially for Callum Bain at number 11 Pine Park, I think it's Silverton Hill, Hamilton in Lancashire.
And before that, something from the new album by the Patti Smith Group, elegantly produced by Todd Rundgren.
The album's called Wave. It's out on Arista, and the track was Revenge.
There's one thing I'd like to say about Patti Smith after seeing her in Germany.
I just wish that Todd Rundgren would produce her stage act.
And here's something by Joe Walsh.
That's a really haunting piece of music by Van Gelis,
and it's the lead track from an album called China.
And the track is called Cheng Kuo, which I believe is Chinese for China,
and The Long March. It's out now on Polidorm.
As indeed is a new biography.
Well, it's not on the record label, but there is a new biography of Chairman Mao out at the moment.
If you're interested in political history, it's really intriguing.
Prior to that, you heard a track by Joe Walsh.
It's a track from an album which has just come out containing the original motion picture soundtrack of a film called The Warriors.
The track was called In the City, and the soundtrack is out on A&M.
It's the only Joe Walsh track on the album.
It's 10.30 here on the Friday Rock Show.
Now let's go back to the 4th of the 1st of 1972.
And here, the first broadcast that was ever put out by Roxy Music.
This was when they had their original line-up.
And before that, Sea Breezes, before that, Would You Believe,
and before that, The Bob, a medley, all put together by Roxy Music
on the 4th of the 1st in 1972 and produced by John Muir.
Now, I said that was the first Roxy Music line-up.
It wasn't, in fact.
It was the first Roxy Music recording line-up.
Now, we're not sure, because it was recorded on a crossover period,
whether that was David O. Liston, the guitar, or Phil Manzanera.
We're not sure. We try to be accurate, but sometimes history defeats us.
On Radio 1 on Bank Holiday Monday,
Phil Leinert of Thin Lizzy talks about his partnership with the Sex Pistols,
his new solo album, his three books of poetry,
and his fascination with the myths and legends of Old Ireland.
An hour of words and music from Phil Leinert.
Bank Holiday Monday, 9pm.
